name: Rauno Freiberg
title: Product Designer
recommendation: Rauno is worth following for how seamlessly he folds motion, hierarchy, and interaction into one cohesive product experience. He is a great reference for learning how to achieve a remarkably polished interface with very little visual noise.

name: Paco Coursey
title: Product Designer
recommendation: Paco is worth studying for the way he compresses complex products into experiences that feel clear, light, and free of unnecessary friction. His work is especially useful for learning how high-density tools become simpler through structure and prioritization.

name: Adam Morse
title: Designer & Developer
recommendation: Adam Morse is worth following because he consistently bridges design and engineering through systems thinking. His work is a strong reference for learning how design language becomes reusable, scalable product infrastructure.

name: Nate Parrott
title: Designer
recommendation: Nate Parrott is worth watching for how he makes AI products feel like real tools instead of concept demos. His work is useful for learning how new technologies can still feel familiar, fast, and well-paced in use.

name: Tobias Fried
title: Product Designer
recommendation: Tobias is worth studying for his ability to keep highly flexible products consistent, quiet, and easy to understand. He is a strong reference for editors, workflows, and multi-state products that need a real sense of order.

name: Diana Mounter
title: Design Infrastructure Lead
recommendation: Diana Mounter is worth following because she represents what it looks like when design systems move from components to organizational capability. Her work is especially valuable for learning consistency, accessibility, and collaboration at scale.

name: Jordan Singer
title: Founder & CEO
recommendation: Jordan Singer is worth following because he is one of the clearest signals for how product design, code, and AI are collapsing into one workflow. His work is especially useful for learning how fast product ideas get shaped, tested, and refined in public.

name: Emil Kowalski
title: Designer & Developer
recommendation: Emil Kowalski is worth following because he treats visual design, interaction, and frontend craft as one integrated discipline. His work is a great reference for seeing how much detail and refinement becomes possible when a designer can also build.

name: Amelia Wattenberger
title: Design Engineer
recommendation: Amelia Wattenberger is worth studying for the way she turns abstract, complex problems into clear interaction structures. Whether in AI interfaces or visualization, her work is an excellent reference for making complexity understandable.

name: Maggie Appleton
title: Product Designer
recommendation: Maggie Appleton is worth following because she is exceptionally good at translating AI capabilities into product experiences people can actually understand and use. Her writing and case studies are especially helpful for learning naming, explanation, and guidance in new interface paradigms.

name: Linus Lee
title: Designer & AI Researcher
recommendation: Linus Lee is worth following because his AI interface experiments do not stop at ideas; they become usable tools. His projects are a great reference for learning new interaction patterns and prototyping instincts in human-AI collaboration.

name: Geoffrey Litt
title: Researcher & Designer
recommendation: Geoffrey Litt is worth studying for his ability to turn research questions into prototypes that people can actually experience. Anyone interested in creative tools, AI assistance, or local-first software can learn a lot from the rigor of his product explorations.

name: Karri Saarinen
title: Co-founder & Designer
recommendation: Karri Saarinen is worth following because he shows how a designer-founder can tie taste, speed, and product judgment together. His work is a great reference for understanding why some tools feel unusually smooth, and how that smoothness is actually designed.

name: Rasmus Andersson
title: Designer & Developer
recommendation: Rasmus Andersson is worth following because his work spans typography, interfaces, and product systems at once. He is a strong reference for learning that design engineering is not just about implementing design, but about deepening and operationalizing design language into infrastructure.

name: Noah Levin
title: VP of Product Design
recommendation: Noah Levin is worth studying for how top design-tool companies maintain clarity and a unified language while the product expands. Following his work is a useful way to learn how design leaders balance systems, innovation, and product cadence.

name: Bret Victor
title: Researcher & Designer
recommendation: Bret Victor is worth following over the long term because his influence is not about a single interface detail, but about how people understand interaction and tools more broadly. If you want to learn foundational design thinking, explainability, and the future of creative tools, he is hard to avoid.

name: Maddie Simens
title: Product Designer
recommendation: Maddie Simens is worth following because her work sits close to the current frontier of AI product execution. She is a useful reference for learning how AI products balance capability, interface clarity, and launch rhythm.

name: Davis Brown
title: Staff Product Designer
recommendation: Davis Brown is worth following because he is designing real generative AI workflows that millions of creative users actually touch. He is a strong reference for learning how frontier AI features become practical, legible product experiences instead of demos.

name: Julie Zhuo
title: VP of Product Design
recommendation: Julie Zhuo is worth following because she has a rare ability to make abstract questions about design growth and management feel concrete and actionable. Beyond product experience, her real value is in helping people build a more mature framework for design judgment.

name: Ryo Lu
title: Head of Design
recommendation: Ryo Lu is worth following because he is shaping one of the most influential AI-native product experiences for builders right now. He is especially useful for learning how speed, taste, and interaction design come together when AI becomes part of the core workflow.

name: Raiza Martin
title: Co-founder & Product Lead
recommendation: Raiza Martin is worth following because she shares from the product edge of AI tools that help people think, learn, and work with knowledge. Her perspective is valuable for understanding how new AI capabilities become repeatable product behavior instead of one-off novelty.

name: Kevin Weil
title: Chief Product Officer
recommendation: Kevin Weil is worth following because he talks from the operating core of one of the most important AI product companies right now. He is a strong reference for learning how frontier model capability gets turned into coherent product direction, prioritization, and distribution.

name: Josh Woodward
title: VP, Gemini and Google Labs
recommendation: Josh Woodward is worth following because he sits close to the launch surface where experimental AI work becomes public product. His updates are especially useful for understanding how large platforms package AI features into things ordinary people will actually try.

name: Cat Wu
title: Claude Code Product Lead
recommendation: Cat Wu is worth following because she is one of the clearest product voices around AI coding tools in practice, not just in theory. Her perspective is valuable for learning how fast feedback loops, user pain, and model capability get translated into product decisions.

name: Nan Yu
title: Head of Product
recommendation: Nan Yu is worth following because he consistently shares from the product layer where AI gets folded into real team workflows. He is a useful reference for learning how PM judgment changes when software becomes faster, more agentic, and less deterministic.

name: Peter Yang
title: Product Leader & Creator
recommendation: Peter Yang is worth following because he translates fast-moving AI product work into practical frameworks that other PMs can actually use. He is especially helpful if you want to keep up with AI product thinking without getting lost in hype or jargon.

name: Nick Turley
title: VP & Head of ChatGPT
recommendation: Nick Turley is worth following because he shares from one of the most important product surfaces in AI right now: ChatGPT itself. His perspective is useful for learning how AI products evolve from features into platforms, and how product bets get framed at very large scale.

name: Aparna Chennapragada
title: Chief Product Officer, AI Experiences
recommendation: Aparna Chennapragada is worth following because she is one of the clearest product leaders thinking about how AI shifts not just features, but the whole product-development model. She is a strong reference for PMs trying to understand agents, prompts, and AI workflows as core product primitives.
